texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SUMMARY
texts.text: SUMMARY

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of chlorinated hydrocarbon vapours through uncrosslinked and crosslinked poly(ethylenecovinyl acetate) (EVA) membranes has been analysed. EVA was crosslinked by dicumyl peroxide and benzoyl peroxide. The uncrosslinked sample showed a lower permeation coeffi  cient due to its crystalline nature. The crystalline nature was supported by X-ray diffraction analysis. The differences in the permeation behaviour of the two crosslinked samples were correlated with crosslink density. Crosslink density was calculated using the equilibrium sorption method. The permeation behaviour is also supported by differential scanning calorimetry. The infl  uence of the crosslink density and the size of penetrants on permeation was analysed. The effect of free volume on the gas barrier properties was investigated by positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS).
texts.text: The permeation of chlorinated hydrocarbon vapours through uncrosslinked and crosslinked poly(ethylenecovinyl acetate) (EVA) membranes has been analysed. EVA was crosslinked by dicumyl peroxide and benzoyl peroxide. The uncrosslinked sample showed a lower permeation coeffi  cient due to its crystalline nature. The crystalline nature was supported by X-ray diffraction analysis. The differences in the permeation behaviour of the two crosslinked samples were correlated with crosslink density. Crosslink density was calculated using the equilibrium sorption method. The permeation behaviour is also supported by differential scanning calorimetry. The infl  uence of the crosslink density and the size of penetrants on permeation was analysed. The effect of free volume on the gas barrier properties was investigated by positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  vapour  permeation  process  is defi  ned  as  the  transport  of  a  condensable vapour  through  a  dense  membrane. This process offers the unique feature of  being  able  to  study  the  transport process of a single permeant through a dense  membrane  under  various  upstream activities 1-3 . Such characteristics cannot be obtained by liquid permeation, where modifi  cation  of  the  upstream  activity of a component can only be achieved by  adding  another  compound  to  the mixture. In this case the activity of both components is modifi  ed in compliance with the Gibbs-Duhem equation, which complicates the transport analysis. On the  contrary,  coupling  phenomena  do not  need  to  be  considered  with  pure vapour permeation results. In the case of pure permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated, provided upstream pressure is precisely monitored 4 .
texts.text: The  vapour  permeation  process  is defi  ned  as  the  transport  of  a  condensable vapour  through  a  dense  membrane. This process offers the unique feature of  being  able  to  study  the  transport process of a single permeant through a dense  membrane  under  various  upstream activities 1-3 . Such characteristics cannot be obtained by liquid permeation, where modifi  cation  of  the  upstream  activity of a component can only be achieved by  adding  another  compound  to  the mixture. In this case the activity of both components is modifi  ed in compliance with the Gibbs-Duhem equation, which complicates the transport analysis. On the  contrary,  coupling  phenomena  do not  need  to  be  considered  with  pure vapour permeation results. In the case of pure permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated, provided upstream pressure is precisely monitored 4 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides  these  advantages,  the  study of  solvent  vapour  permeation  offers direct  practical  conclusions  for  the understanding of and rational design for  vapour  recovery  of  volatile organic  components  (VOC)  from a  contaminated  air  stream 5 .  Vapour permeation  also  offers  significant opportunities  for  energy  saving  and solvent  reuse,  compared  to  classical VOC  control  processes  such  as incineration,  oxidation  or  active  carbon adsorption.
texts.text: Besides  these  advantages,  the  study of  solvent  vapour  permeation  offers direct  practical  conclusions  for  the understanding of and rational design for  vapour  recovery  of  volatile organic  components  (VOC)  from a  contaminated  air  stream 5 .  Vapour permeation  also  offers  significant opportunities  for  energy  saving  and solvent  reuse,  compared  to  classical VOC  control  processes  such  as incineration,  oxidation  or  active  carbon adsorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vapour  permeation  is  based  on  the vapour  permeability  and  partial pressure difference between the feed side  and  permeate  side  streams 6 .  A method  was  developed  for  simultaneous determination  of  the  permeability, diffusion  and  solubility  coeffi  cients of organic vapours through polymers from the  mass  transfer  through  polymer membranes. The sorption and diffusion coeffi  cients  of  acetylene,  ethylene,

texts.orig: ethane, propane, butane, pentane and xenon  in  poly(trimethylvinylsilane) were  determined  by  a  sorptiondesorption  method 7 .  Sansone  and Tewari 8 calculated  the  permeability coeffi  cient  of  vapours  of  benzene in  air  from  the  measured  values  of solubility and diffusivity. Yampolskii et al. 9 reported the thermodynamics of gas and vapour sorption by amorphous glassy Tefl  on compounds. They found that the vapour solubility coeffi  cient increases  as  the  content  of  the  monomer increases. Fornasiero et al. 10 reported the vapour sorption characteristics of 4-vinylpyridine-based copolymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poly(ethylenecovinyl  acetate)  (EV A) is  a  random  copolymer  of  ethylene and  vinyl  acetate  which  fi  nds  many applications. A wide range of physical properties  can  be  obtained  by  adjusting the  copolymer  composition.  The polymer is extensively used in many engineering  and  industrial  areas because  of  its  toughness,  chemical resistance,  intrinsic  fl  exibility,  excellent processability, etc. The copolymer has been used in many applications due to its  chemical  stability,  biocompatibility

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Materials
texts.text: 2.1 Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poly(ethyleneco -vinyl  acetate) (EVA;  Pilene  1802)  was  supplied by  Polyolefin  Industries  Limited (PIL),  Chennai,  India.  The  basic characteristics  of  the  copolymer  are given  in Table  1 .  The  crosslinking agents  used  were  dicumyl  peroxide (DCP)  and  benzoyl  peroxide  (BP). The solvents used, CCl 4 ,  CHCl 3 and CH 2 Cl 2 , were of reagent grade.
texts.text: Poly(ethyleneco -vinyl  acetate) (EVA;  Pilene  1802)  was  supplied by  Polyolefin  Industries  Limited (PIL),  Chennai,  India.  The  basic characteristics  of  the  copolymer  are given  in Table  1 .  The  crosslinking agents  used  were  dicumyl  peroxide (DCP)  and  benzoyl  peroxide  (BP). The solvents used, CCl 4 ,  CHCl 3 and CH 2 Cl 2 , were of reagent grade.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Membrane Preparation
texts.text: 2.2 Membrane Preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes of EVA were fabricated by  using  two  crosslinking  agents, namely  DCP  and  BP.  Unmodifi  ed EVA  (neat)  membranes  were  also prepared.  The  unmodifi  ed membranes are represented as D 0 . EVA granules were sheeted out on a two-roll mixing mill  with  a  friction  ratio  1:1.4.  The sheeted-out  stock  was  pressed  on hydraulic press at 170 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N. The modifi  ed membranes were prepared using BP and DCP. The mixing was done on a two-roll  mixing  mill  as  before.  The cure  behaviour  was  studied  with  a

The  unmodifi  ed membranes are represented as D 0 . EVA granules were sheeted out on a two-roll mixing mill  with  a  friction  ratio  1:1.4.  The sheeted-out  stock  was  pressed  on hydraulic press at 170 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N. The modifi  ed membranes were prepared using BP and DCP. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Geottfert  rheometer.  The  samples  were then cured up to their optimum cure time. DCP-modifi  ed membranes were cured at 170 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N (represented as D). The BP-modifi  ed membrane was cured at 120 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N (represented as B 1 ). Membranes with different loadings of DCP were also prepared. DCP-modifi  ed membranes are designated as D 1 ,  D 2 ,  D 4 ,  D 6 and D 8 according to the DCP content, where the  subscript  numbers  represent  the grams of DCP used per 100 g of the polymer. Similarly,  the  BP-modifi  ed membrane  is  represented  as  B 1 , according to the BP content.
texts.text: Geottfert  rheometer.  The  samples  were then cured up to their optimum cure time. DCP-modifi  ed membranes were cured at 170 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N (represented as D). The BP-modifi  ed membrane was cured at 120 °C and under a load of 24.5 × 10 3 N (represented as B 1 ). Membranes with different loadings of DCP were also prepared. DCP-modifi  ed membranes are designated as D 1 ,  D 2 ,  D 4 ,  D 6 and D 8 according to the DCP content, where the  subscript  numbers  represent  the grams of DCP used per 100 g of the polymer. Similarly,  the  BP-modifi  ed membrane  is  represented  as  B 1 , according to the BP content.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3 Vapour Permeation
texts.text: 2.3 Vapour Permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  vapour  permeability  was determined  at  room  temperature  by measuring the  weight  loss  of  small  vials fi  lled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane of 100 μ m thickness. The  weight  loss  is  proportional  to time,  the  area  of  the  membrane  and the  pressure  inside  the  vials,  and  is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.
texts.text: The  vapour  permeability  was determined  at  room  temperature  by measuring the  weight  loss  of  small  vials fi  lled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane of 100 μ m thickness. The  weight  loss  is  proportional  to time,  the  area  of  the  membrane  and the  pressure  inside  the  vials,  and  is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4 Sorption Experiments
texts.text: 2.4 Sorption Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  EVA  samples  for  sorption experiments  (ASTM  D-471)  were punched  out  in  circular  shape  of diameter 19 mm from tensile sheets and dried in a vacuum desiccator over anhydrous CaCl 2 at room temperature for  about  24  h.  The  original  weight and  thickness  of  the  samples  were measured. They were then immersed in  chloroform  (15-20  ml)  in  closed diffusion bottles, and kept at constant temperature  in  an  air  oven  till equilibrium was attained.  The samples were removed from the bottles, dried for  5-10  s  between  fi  lter  papers  to remove  the  excess  solvent  on  their surface  and  weighed  instantly  in  an electronic balance (Shimadzu, Libror AEU-210,  Japan)  that  measured reproducibly within ± 0.0001 g.
texts.text: The  EVA  samples  for  sorption experiments  (ASTM  D-471)  were punched  out  in  circular  shape  of diameter 19 mm from tensile sheets and dried in a vacuum desiccator over anhydrous CaCl 2 at room temperature for  about  24  h.  The  original  weight and  thickness  of  the  samples  were measured. They were then immersed in  chloroform  (15-20  ml)  in  closed diffusion bottles, and kept at constant temperature  in  an  air  oven  till equilibrium was attained.  The samples were removed from the bottles, dried for  5-10  s  between  fi  lter  papers  to remove  the  excess  solvent  on  their surface  and  weighed  instantly  in  an electronic balance (Shimadzu, Libror AEU-210,  Japan)  that  measured reproducibly within ± 0.0001 g.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5 X-Ray Diffraction Analysis
texts.text: 2.5 X-Ray Diffraction Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  patterns were taken by using Ni-fi  ltered Cu K α radiation ( λ = 0.154 nm) on an X'pert diffractometer (Philips) at 40 keV and 30 mA. The operating voltage and the current of the tube were kept the same throughout the investigation.
texts.text: X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  patterns were taken by using Ni-fi  ltered Cu K α radiation ( λ = 0.154 nm) on an X'pert diffractometer (Philips) at 40 keV and 30 mA. The operating voltage and the current of the tube were kept the same throughout the investigation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.6 Differential Scanning Calorimetric Analysis
texts.text: 2.6 Differential Scanning Calorimetric Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermograms of fi  lms were recorded using  a  Q10  model  TA  instrument to  determine  the  glass  transition temperature.  The  heating  rate  used was 10 °C/min and the purge gas was nitrogen. The samples were analysed from -50 °C to +200 °C.
texts.text: Thermograms of fi  lms were recorded using  a  Q10  model  TA  instrument to  determine  the  glass  transition temperature.  The  heating  rate  used was 10 °C/min and the purge gas was nitrogen. The samples were analysed from -50 °C to +200 °C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.7 Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopic Analysis
texts.text: 2.7 Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopic Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Positron  annihilation  lifetime spectroscopy  (PALS)  is  used  to examine  the  free  volume  present  in EVA samples. The  positron  lifetime spectrometer  consists  of  a  fastfast  coincidence  system  with  BaF 2 scintillators  coupled  to  photomultiplier tubes of type XP2020/Q with quartz window  as  detectors.  The  detectors were  conically  shaped  to  achieve better  time  resolution.  A  17  mCi 22 Na  positron  source,  deposited  on a  pure  Kapton  foil  of  0.0127  mm thickness,  was  placed  between  two identical pieces of the sample under investigation.  This  sample-source sandwich was positioned between the two PALS detectors to acquire lifetime spectrum. The spectrometer measures 180 ps as the resolution function with 60 Co source. However, for better count rate, the spectrometer was operated at 220 ps time resolution 11 . All lifetime measurements were performed at room
texts.text: Positron  annihilation  lifetime spectroscopy  (PALS)  is  used  to examine  the  free  volume  present  in EVA samples. The  positron  lifetime spectrometer  consists  of  a  fastfast  coincidence  system  with  BaF 2 scintillators  coupled  to  photomultiplier tubes of type XP2020/Q with quartz window  as  detectors.  The  detectors were  conically  shaped  to  achieve better  time  resolution.  A  17  mCi 22 Na  positron  source,  deposited  on a  pure  Kapton  foil  of  0.0127  mm thickness,  was  placed  between  two identical pieces of the sample under investigation.  This  sample-source sandwich was positioned between the two PALS detectors to acquire lifetime spectrum. The spectrometer measures 180 ps as the resolution function with 60 Co source. However, for better count rate, the spectrometer was operated at 220 ps time resolution 11 . All lifetime measurements were performed at room

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. THEORY
texts.text: 3. THEORY

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 PALS Measurements
texts.text: 3.1 PALS Measurements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  P ALS  analysis  there  are  two  measured parameters,  namely  o-Ps  lifetime  ( τ 3 ) and o-Ps intensity I 3 . The o-Ps lifetime τ 3 measures the size of the free volume holes ( V f )  and I 3 is  a  relative  measure of the number of free volume sites in the polymer matrix. The free volume cavity  radius  ( R )  is  related  to  the o-Ps pick-off lifetime ( τ 3 ) by a simple relation. The underlying assumption in the formulation of this relation is that  an  o-Ps  atom  in  a  free  volume cell can be approximated to a particle in a potential well of radius R 0 . The potential  is  infi  nite  if r > R 0 and  constant for r ≤ R 0 . Further, it is assumed that there  is  an  electron  layer  in  the region R < r < R 0 , with R 0 = R + δ R , where δ R represents the thickness of the electron layer or the probability of  overlap  of  the  Ps  wavefunction and the electron wavefunction. The expression connecting the free volume radius R (in nm) and the o-Ps pickoff  lifetime τ 3 (in  ns)  according  to Nakanishi et al. 12 is
texts.text: In  P ALS  analysis  there  are  two  measured parameters,  namely  o-Ps  lifetime  ( τ 3 ) and o-Ps intensity I 3 . The o-Ps lifetime τ 3 measures the size of the free volume holes ( V f )  and I 3 is  a  relative  measure of the number of free volume sites in the polymer matrix. The free volume cavity  radius  ( R )  is  related  to  the o-Ps pick-off lifetime ( τ 3 ) by a simple relation. The underlying assumption in the formulation of this relation is that  an  o-Ps  atom  in  a  free  volume cell can be approximated to a particle in a potential well of radius R 0 . The potential  is  infi  nite  if r > R 0 and  constant for r ≤ R 0 . Further, it is assumed that there  is  an  electron  layer  in  the region R < r < R 0 , with R 0 = R + δ R , where δ R represents the thickness of the electron layer or the probability of  overlap  of  the  Ps  wavefunction and the electron wavefunction. The expression connecting the free volume radius R (in nm) and the o-Ps pickoff  lifetime τ 3 (in  ns)  according  to Nakanishi et al. 12 is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here,  the  value  of δ R =  0.1656  nm was determined by fi  tting  experimental τ 3 values  to  data  from  molecular materials with well-known hole size, like  zeolites.  Using  this  value  of R , the free volume size ( V f ) is calculated as V f =  (4/3) R 3 .  Then  the  relative fractional free volume is evaluated as the  product  of  free  volume  ( V f )  and o-Ps intensity, I 3 (%).
texts.text: Here,  the  value  of δ R =  0.1656  nm was determined by fi  tting  experimental τ 3 values  to  data  from  molecular materials with well-known hole size, like  zeolites.  Using  this  value  of R , the free volume size ( V f ) is calculated as V f =  (4/3) R 3 .  Then  the  relative fractional free volume is evaluated as the  product  of  free  volume  ( V f )  and o-Ps intensity, I 3 (%).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
texts.text: 4. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopic (PALS) Analysis
texts.text: 4.1 Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopic (PALS) Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An  understanding  of  the  free volume  of  polymers  is  crucial  for determining  their  permeability and  ionic  conductivity.  Nowadays, PALS analysis is carried out for the experimental  quantifi  cation  of  free volume 14,15 .  It  has  been  found  that the  estimated  hole  size  signifi  cantly relates to the free volume property of polymer materials.  Ito et  al. 16 examined the  relationship  between the oxygen permeability and the free volume for ethylene-vinyl  alcohol  copolymer, which signifi  cantly followed the free volume theory as the ethylene content varied. The results obtained by them suggest that the molecular mechanism of gas permeation could be considered on  the  basis  of  local  motion  of  the polymer  segments  and  free  volume size. Nagel et al. 17 correlated the free volume  and  transport  properties  of highly selective polymer membranes. Free  volume  values  obtained  for various  EVA  samples  are  shown  in Table 2 .
texts.text: An  understanding  of  the  free volume  of  polymers  is  crucial  for determining  their  permeability and  ionic  conductivity.  Nowadays, PALS analysis is carried out for the experimental  quantifi  cation  of  free volume 14,15 .  It  has  been  found  that the  estimated  hole  size  signifi  cantly relates to the free volume property of polymer materials.  Ito et  al. 16 examined the  relationship  between the oxygen permeability and the free volume for ethylene-vinyl  alcohol  copolymer, which signifi  cantly followed the free volume theory as the ethylene content varied. The results obtained by them suggest that the molecular mechanism of gas permeation could be considered on  the  basis  of  local  motion  of  the polymer  segments  and  free  volume size. Nagel et al. 17 correlated the free volume  and  transport  properties  of highly selective polymer membranes. Free  volume  values  obtained  for various  EVA  samples  are  shown  in Table 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Table 2 it  can  be  inferred  that  the uncrosslinked sample (D 0 ) exhibits a lower relative fractional free volume compared to B 1 and D 1 . The low value is  due  to  the  presence  of  crystalline regions  in  the  matrix.  When  BP  or DCP was introduced, the crystallinity was  reduced  due  to  the  presence  of random  C-C  crosslinks.  The  DCPcured  sample  exhibited  lower  fractional free  volume percentage value ( F v %) than  B 1 .  Hence  at  the  same  loading of  DCP/BP,  maximum  crosslinks were  generated  by  DCP,  and  hence the DCP-cured samples exhibit lower F v % values. Among the various DCP crosslinked  samples, F v %  decreases from D 1 to D 8 . As the amount of DCP increases,  the  number  of  crosslinks per unit volume increases and hence the free volume decreases.
texts.text: From Table 2 it  can  be  inferred  that  the uncrosslinked sample (D 0 ) exhibits a lower relative fractional free volume compared to B 1 and D 1 . The low value is  due  to  the  presence  of  crystalline regions  in  the  matrix.  When  BP  or DCP was introduced, the crystallinity was  reduced  due  to  the  presence  of random  C-C  crosslinks.  The  DCPcured  sample  exhibited  lower  fractional free  volume percentage value ( F v %) than  B 1 .  Hence  at  the  same  loading of  DCP/BP,  maximum  crosslinks were  generated  by  DCP,  and  hence the DCP-cured samples exhibit lower F v % values. Among the various DCP crosslinked  samples, F v %  decreases from D 1 to D 8 . As the amount of DCP increases,  the  number  of  crosslinks per unit volume increases and hence the free volume decreases.

texts.orig: The calculated values of the crosslink density  of  B 1 and  D 1 samples  are 1.02 × 10 -5 and  2.92 × 10 -5 mol/cm 3 , respectively. In the present case, the amount  of  solvent  absorbed  by  the D 1 sample  decreases  and  hence  the φ value  increases,  leading  to  higher crosslink  density.  The  increase  in crosslink  density  suggests  that  the maximum number  of  crosslinks  per unit volume is possessed by the DCP vulcanised  sample.  Higher  crosslink density values mean more restraint on the network and this results in lower swelling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The crosslinked samples (D 1 and B 1 ) showed the highest vapour permeation due to the increase in free volume of the  matrix.  When  EV A  was  crosslinked by DCP or BP, the crystallinity was reduced due to the formation of C-C networks.  The  above  situation  is schematically represented in Figure 3 . When the crystalline  fraction  of  the polymer decreases, there is an increase in  the  volume  of  the  amorphous fraction  and  in  chain  lengths  that connect  the  crystalline  domains.  A higher material volume accessible for vapour sorption and a higher fl  exibility of the network allow one to predict the increased transport of vapours and a higher  permeation. The reduction in crystallinity  is  supported  by  X-ray diffraction  patterns.  The  modified membrane showed a less intense peak than the unmodifi  ed one. PALS results given in Table 2 show  that  the  free volume of the uncrosslinked polymer increases on addition of DCP or BP.

A higher material volume accessible for vapour sorption and a higher fl  exibility of the network allow one to predict the increased transport of vapours and a higher  permeation. The reduction in crystallinity  is  supported  by  X-ray diffraction  patterns.  The  modified membrane showed a less intense peak than the unmodifi  ed one. PALS results given in Table 2 show  that  the  free volume of the uncrosslinked polymer increases on addition of DCP or BP.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.5 Infl  uence of Crosslinker Loading on Vapour Permeation
texts.text: 4.5 Infl  uence of Crosslinker Loading on Vapour Permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 4 shows  the  effect  of  crosslinker loading on the vapour permeability of EVA membranes. It can be seen that, as the amount of crosslinker increases, the  vapour  permeability  decreases. When the amount of DCP increases, the  polymer  matrix  contains  a  large number of rigid C-C linkages which do  not  allow  the  chain  to  rearrange

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.6 Infl  uence of Penetrant Size on Vapour Permeation
texts.text: 4.6 Infl  uence of Penetrant Size on Vapour Permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  influence  of  the  nature  of penetrants on vapour transport  is  given in Figure 5 . The membrane used was a crosslinked EVA membrane.  Among the  three  chlorinated  hydrocarbons, higher  vapour  permeability  is  observed for chloroform compared to the other solvents, viz .  CCl 4 and  CH 2 Cl 2 .  The transport  of  vapour  depends  upon various  factors,  which  include  the molecular size of the penetrant and its interaction with the matrix. In the case of  chlorinated  hydrocarbons,  the  extent of  matrix-penetrant  interaction  has been found to  outweigh  the  infl  uence  of molecular size.  The above observation can be explained on the basis of the solubility parameter values. EVA and CHCl 3 have  closer  solubility  parameter values  compared  to  other  systems ( δ of  EV A  and  CHCl 3 are  18.8  and 19  MPa 1/2 respectively).  The  closer the solubility parameters, the greater will be the solubility of the polymer in the solvent 22 .
texts.text: The  influence  of  the  nature  of penetrants on vapour transport  is  given in Figure 5 . The membrane used was a crosslinked EVA membrane.  Among the  three  chlorinated  hydrocarbons, higher  vapour  permeability  is  observed for chloroform compared to the other solvents, viz .  CCl 4 and  CH 2 Cl 2 .  The transport  of  vapour  depends  upon various  factors,  which  include  the molecular size of the penetrant and its interaction with the matrix. In the case of  chlorinated  hydrocarbons,  the  extent of  matrix-penetrant  interaction  has been found to  outweigh  the  infl  uence  of molecular size.  The above observation can be explained on the basis of the solubility parameter values. EVA and CHCl 3 have  closer  solubility  parameter values  compared  to  other  systems ( δ of  EV A  and  CHCl 3 are  18.8  and 19  MPa 1/2 respectively).  The  closer the solubility parameters, the greater will be the solubility of the polymer in the solvent 22 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: 5. CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  transport  of chlorinated hydrocarbon  vapours  through  EVA membranes  has  shown  that  the permeation behaviour varies  according to  the  structure  of  the  material. Uncrosslinked  EVA  membranes showed the least permeability. When crosslinked  by  dicumyl  peroxide  or benzoyl peroxide, vapour permeability was increased due to reduction in the crystallinity  of  the  matrix.  Positron annihilation  lifetime  spectroscopic analysis has shown that the fractional free  volume percentage is minimum for  the  uncrosslinked  sample  and that  the  value  increases  on  addition of  dicumyl  peroxide  or  benzoyl peroxide.  EV A  membranes  crosslinked by  benzoyl  peroxide  showed  the maximum  vapour  permeability. The  difference  in  the  behaviour of  benzoyl  peroxide  and  dicumyl peroxide crosslinked membranes was supported by thermograms. It is also observed that crosslinker loading has a signifi  cant infl  uence on permeation behaviour.  Among  the  penetrants chloroform  showed  the  maximum vapour permeability.
texts.text: The  transport  of chlorinated hydrocarbon  vapours  through  EVA membranes  has  shown  that  the permeation behaviour varies  according to  the  structure  of  the  material. Uncrosslinked  EVA  membranes showed the least permeability. When crosslinked  by  dicumyl  peroxide  or benzoyl peroxide, vapour permeability was increased due to reduction in the crystallinity  of  the  matrix.  Positron annihilation  lifetime  spectroscopic analysis has shown that the fractional free  volume percentage is minimum for  the  uncrosslinked  sample  and that  the  value  increases  on  addition of  dicumyl  peroxide  or  benzoyl peroxide.  EV A  membranes  crosslinked by  benzoyl  peroxide  showed  the maximum  vapour  permeability. The  difference  in  the  behaviour of  benzoyl  peroxide  and  dicumyl peroxide crosslinked membranes was supported by thermograms. It is also observed that crosslinker loading has a signifi  cant infl  uence on permeation behaviour.  Among  the  penetrants chloroform  showed  the  maximum vapour permeability.
